content.text: Now let me check the FakeWebHostEnvironment pattern and look at the dev diary / task to understand context:

Development Diary

1. Ran the Engin harness orchestrator (my own: https://github.com/harms-haus/engin) to generate a base to start with.
2. Human Review: Fixed a few issues. Added icon pack (bootstrap). Cleaned up the dialog styling. Added an embedded mode so that the file browser appears in the browser's main page. The button remains and continues to open a dialog version. Added a sample set of files to browse around in.

# Software Developer Test Project 2026

## Project Goals

- **Spend a few hours:** Put your best foot forward, submit a project that shows your best work. The evaluation will be code reviews and discussions of your implementation and architecture choices.

- **Download the empty project:** [Here](#). We have started you with an index.html file and an empty controller. When you are done you can zip it up and share it with the team for review either as a download link or zip attachment to an email. If you share the project by email keep in mind the zip has exe files inside it and Gmail and other virus scanners may block the email. To avoid issues, rename the extension to something like `MyProject.CHANGEME`

- **End-to-End Flow that Works:** Implement both server side and client side logic in a primitive end to end flow that runs without errors for a few small features. The key goal is to show a working end to end proof of concept with enough C# and JavaScript code that we can get a feel for your coding style and have some interesting classes to discuss in the code review.

  - **Simple is better:** The less boilerplate junk the better - if you can pick a barebones project type or delete all the default controllers and files you are not using it will help us focus on your code

  - **Focus on Code:** Spend most of your time actually writing code so that we can have a substantial work product to review. For the purpose of this exercise we really need to see original code and are not interested in seeing a lot of framework or template usage.

## File & Directory Browsing Single Page App

The task is to create a web service API that allows users to query the contents of a directory on the web server and a single page web app that can be used to search and browse folders and files. A server side home directory should be configurable via variable.

Client side, the application should be deep-linkable (the state of the UI should be kept in the URL). All of the UI work should be done client side via JavaScript that renders HTML - do not render HTML server side. Styling is not as important for this task, we are more concerned with functionality. Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library).

The goal is to produce a functional proof-of-concept to let us see how you think. It is important to provide a decent body of code, so that we can talk about your logic and choices. It isn't important to make the UI "pretty".

The point of this exercise is to end up with a body of work that is big enough that our team can review it and evaluate your design and coding style and skills.

## Requirements

- Ensure your solution builds in Visual Studio (any version 2022 or newer is acceptable), Rider, VS Code, or command line SDK tools
- Web API that allows Browse and Search Files & Folders and returns JSON
- Deep linkable URL pattern
- SPA (Single Page App using JavaScript)
- Upload/download files from the browser
- Show file and folder counts and sizes for the current view
- Build the UI using vanilla JavaScript or TypeScript (without React, Angular, or other UI library)

## Bonus

- Entire component contained in a dialog widget, with a trigger element (button, etc)
- Delete, move, copy files and folders
- Highly performant

content.thinking:
Interesting! Let me check the directory sort more carefully:

`{ "beta", "alpha", "Gamma" }` → `{ "alpha", "beta", "Gamma" }`

Wait, that's "alpha" (a=97), "beta" (b=98), "Gamma" (G=71 in ASCII but OrdinalIgnoreCase lowercases to 'g'=103).

So with OrdinalIgnoreCase: "alpha" → "alpha" (a=97), "beta" → "beta" (b=98), "Gamma" → "gamma" (g=103).

So order: alpha (97) < beta (98) < Gamma/gamma (103).

I was wrong earlier! OrdinalIgnoreCase converts both to uppercase (or lowercase — same result) before comparing using ordinal. So 'G' → 'g' (103), not the uppercase 'G' (71).

So `{ "beta", "alpha", "Gamma" }` sorted OrdinalIgnoreCase → `{ "alpha", "beta", "Gamma" }`.

Let me update my test expectations. The sort is by the lowercased (or uppercased) first character:
- alpha: a=97
- beta: b=98
- Gamma: G→g=103

So sorted: alpha, beta, Gamma.

Let me verify with a quick test.

Now trailing slash: On Linux, `Path.GetFullPath("/tmp/reftest/docs/")` actually KEEPS the trailing slash! That's interesting. So `Path.Combine(root, "docs/")` → "/tmp/reftest/docs/" and `GetFullPath` keeps the trailing slash.

Wait — but the actual `SafeResolve` does:
```
var full = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(root, relativePath));
var isRoot = string.Equals(full, root, StringComparison.Ordinal);
var isInsideRoot = full.StartsWith(root + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar, StringComparison.Ordinal);
```

If full = "/tmp/reftest/docs/" and root = "/tmp/reftest/Home", then isInsideRoot would be checking if "/tmp/reftest/docs/" starts with "/tmp/reftest/Home/" — that depends on the root name. In the test the home is "Home" so root = "/tmp/reftest/Home". Then `isInsideRoot` checks if full starts with "/tmp/reftest/Home/" — yes since "/tmp/reftest/docs/" starts with "/tmp/reftest/Home/"... wait no, that's not right. "/tmp/reftest/docs/" does NOT start with "/tmp/reftest/Home/" because "docs/" vs "Home/" differ at char 14.

Hmm wait, let me reconsider. In the test, `Path.Combine(_contentRoot, "docs/")` would actually be relative to the content root, not the home root. SafeResolve combines relativePath with the *home root*, not the content root. Let me re-read.

```csharp
private string SafeResolve(string relativePath)
{
    var root = Root;  // = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(contentRoot, "Home"))
    ...
    var full = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(root, relativePath));
```

So `root` is the home root. `relativePath` "docs/" gets combined with root. So `full = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine("/path/to/Home", "docs/"))`.

If root = "/tmp/x/Home" and relativePath = "docs/", then `Path.Combine("/tmp/x/Home", "docs/")` = "/tmp/x/Home/docs/". `GetFullPath` = "/tmp/x/Home/docs/" (trailing slash kept on Linux per my test).

Then `isInsideRoot` = "/tmp/x/Home/docs/".StartsWith("/tmp/x/Home/") = true. Good.

Now `ToRelative`:
```csharp
return full
    .Substring(root.Length)
    .TrimStart(Path.DirectorySeparatorChar, Path.AltDirectorySeparatorChar)
    .Replace('\\', '/');
```

`full = "/tmp/x/Home/docs/"`, `root = "/tmp/x/Home"`, `root.Length = 13`.
`full.Substring(13)` = "/docs/" → TrimStart('/') → "docs/" → Replace('\\','/') → "docs/".

So the relative path becomes "docs/" with trailing slash. Hmm! That's a bit weird but it's the existing behavior. Then `ComputeParent("docs/")`:
```csharp
var separatorIndex = relative.LastIndexOf('/');  // = 4 (the trailing slash)
if (separatorIndex < 0) return string.Empty;
return relative.Substring(0, separatorIndex);  // = "docs"
```

So Parent would be "docs". But the path is "docs/".

This is messy and probably platform-dependent (Windows would trim the trailing slash). Let me NOT write a test for trailing slashes since the behavior is platform-dependent and weird. The test would be fragile.

Actually wait — let me re-verify. Let me test directly.

content.thinking:
Ah, of course. The `Home` directory is created lazily on first use. The tests that call `File.WriteAllTextAsync` *before* calling `Browse` will fail because the directory doesn't exist yet. The existing tests that write files before calling Browse either:
- Use `Directory.CreateDirectory` first (which creates the parent), OR
- Call `service.ResolveFullPath("")` first to materialize the root.

Looking at the existing tests:
- `Browse_Root_ReturnsDirectoriesFirstThenFiles_SortedOrdinalIgnoreCase`: uses `Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.Combine(root, "images"))` first — this creates `root` as a side effect. Then `File.WriteAllTextAsync(Path.Combine(root, "z.txt"), "ZZ")` works because `root` now exists.

So when my test only writes files (without creating any directory first), the `root` directory doesn't exist yet. I need to either:
1. Create the root directory explicitly first, OR
2. Call `service.ResolveFullPath("")` first to materialize it, OR
3. Use `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)` at the start.

The cleanest fix: call `service.ResolveFullPath("")` at the start of each affected test to materialize the lazy root. Or use `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)`.

Actually the existing pattern in some tests is to call `service.ResolveFullPath("")` to trigger lazy init (see `Search_CapsResultsAtFiveHundred`, `Delete_RemovesFile`, etc.). Let me use that.

For the tests that ONLY create files (no Directory.CreateDirectory calls), I need to materialize the root first. Let me fix:
- `Browse_DirectoryWithOnlyFiles_SortsByName_WithZeroFolderCount`
- `Browse_SortsEntries_OrdinalIgnoreCase_MixedCaseNames`
- `Browse_ZeroByteFile_ReportsSizeZero`
- `Browse_PreservesEntryNames_AndPaths_ForSpacesDotsAndNoExtension`
- `Browse_TotalSize_SumsAllCurrentDirectoryFileSizes` (this one creates a subdir, so root is created as side effect — should be OK, but let me check)

Wait, `Browse_TotalSize_SumsAllCurrentDirectoryFileSizes` does `Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.Combine(root, "subdir"))` which would create `root` as side effect. So that one should be OK.

Let me check which tests failed:
- Browse_SortsEntries_OrdinalIgnoreCase_MixedCaseNames (only files)
- Browse_DirectoryWithOnlyFiles_SortsByName_WithZeroFolderCount (only files)
